Author: Iamabdullah
Title: GitHub Copilot: Under the Hood and Into Production
Text:
GitHub Copilot: Under the Hood and Into Production
Most engineers use GitHub Copilot the same way they use autocomplete on their phone: accept whatever shows up first and move on. They leave 60–70% of its capability on the table because they never learn how the tool decides what to suggest. The engineers who get 10x value from Copilot are not smarter. They are more intentional about context, prompts, and configuration.
This article breaks down how Copilot works at a technical level and gives you concrete techniques to make it produce significantly better output starting today.
How GitHub Copilot Actually Works
Copilot is not a search engine. It does not look up code from a database and paste it into your editor. It is a next-token predictor: a large language model trained on code that generates the most probable sequence of tokens given the context you provide.
When you type a character in your editor, a multi-step pipeline executes before a suggestion appears. First, the IDE plugin detects your keystroke and waits for a brief debounce period, roughly 75 milliseconds, to batch rapid inputs. Then Copilot extracts two key pieces: the prefix (all code before your cursor) and the suffix (all code after it). If code exists on both sides of the cursor, Copilot switches to Fill-in-the-Middle (FIM) mode. FIM tells the model to generate code that fits between the prefix and suffix, giving it clearer intent about what belongs in that gap. It is like filling a blank in a printed form: the surrounding text defines the constraints, and only one kind of answer fits cleanly.
Next comes context retrieval. Copilot scans all open editor tabs, recently edited files, files in the same directory, and your import graph. It breaks each file into 60-line sliding windows and uses Jaccard similarity, a fast token-overlap algorithm, to score how relevant each window is to the code surrounding your cursor. Only the highest-scoring window per file survives. For workspace-wide search, it uses vector embeddings (a lightweight RAG pipeline) to find semantically similar code across the full project.
The Prompt Assembler then builds the final prompt: prefix, suffix, ranked snippets from neighbouring files, imports, constants, and the contents of your copilot-instructions.md file. It continuously checks the token budget and trims lower-priority content when the limit approaches. The assembled prompt is sent to the model (GPT-5.3-Codex, Opus, Sonnet, Gemini, depending on your configuration), and suggestions stream back as ghost text in your editor. Copilot generates 1–3 alternatives for inline mode and up to 10 in the Suggestions Panel.
Structure your files and tab arrangement around this: every piece of context that reaches the model influences the output. The code before your cursor has the highest weight. Open tabs are scored by textual similarity to your current code. Files with naming patterns and variable names that match yours rank higher. If you have 20 irrelevant files open from a prior debugging session, the model’s limited context window gets filled with noise, and suggestion quality drops accordingly.
Why Your Copilot Suggestions Are Generic (And the Exact Files Causing It)
The single most impactful change you will make to Copilot’s output quality is improving the context it receives. The model generates predictions based on everything it sees. More relevant context, higher quality suggestions. It is a direct, measurable relationship.
Before you start coding on a feature, open every file related to the task: the file you are editing, the type definitions or interfaces you reference, similar components that follow the pattern you want, API service files your code will call, and model or schema files that define data structures. Copilot processes all open tabs and ranks them by relevance to your cursor position. When those tabs contain code that shares naming conventions, types, and patterns with what you are writing, suggestions align with your architecture instead of generating generic boilerplate.
Close every file that is not part of the current task. Copilot’s context window has a fixed token budget, and every open file competes for space in it. Irrelevant files dilute every suggestion.
Before writing a function, declare your imports. When Copilot sees which libraries you are using, it suggests code that calls those exact APIs instead of inventing a different approach. If you import express, it will suggest Express middleware patterns. If you import fastapi, it will suggest FastAPI route handlers. The import section is a high-priority signal.
Place detailed comments directly above function signatures. Comments in this position carry the strongest influence on suggestions. They act as a specification: what the function does, what it returns, what constraints apply.
Here is a concrete example. A vague comment:
# get tasks
# Produces a generic function that returns a list, likely with no filtering, no pagination, and no error handling. Compare that with a structured docstring placed before the function body:
def get_tasks_by_user(user_id: int, status: str = None) -> list[TaskResponse]:
"""
Retrieve all tasks for a user, optionally filtered by status.
Args:
user_id: The ID of the authenticated user.
status: Optional filter. One of: "pending", "in_progress", "done".
Returns:
A list of TaskResponse objects, ordered by created_at descending.
Raises:
HTTPException: 404 if the user does not exist.
HTTPException: 422 if status is not a valid value.
"""
This produces a function that queries by user_id, applies the status filter with validation, returns the correct Pydantic schema, and raises the exact HTTP exceptions defined in the docstring.
Set up a .github/copilot-instructions.md file in your repository. Copilot reads this file automatically on every request. Think of it as onboarding documentation written specifically for your AI pair programmer. Include your tech stack, coding patterns, naming conventions, testing framework, error handling approach, and security rules. Here is a real template:
Project Instructions
Tech Stack
- Backend: FastAPI + Python 3.12 + SQLAlchemy (async) + PostgreSQL
- Validation: Pydantic v2 for all request and response schemas
- Testing: pytest + pytest-asyncio + httpx AsyncClient
- Database migrations: Alembic
Code Patterns
- All route handlers are async def
- Business logic lives in service modules, not in routers
- Use Pydantic models for all input validation and response serialization
- Type-annotate every function signature, including return types
- Google-style docstrings on all public functions with Args, Returns, Raises
Testing
- Tests mirror the src/ structure under tests/
- Use descriptive names: test_returns_404_when_task_not_found
- Fixture setup and db session patching live in conftest.py
- Mock external services with pytest-mock
Error Handling
- Raise HTTPException with explicit status_code and detail string
- Service layer raises ValueError with field-specific messages
- All database calls wrapped in try/except with rollback on failure
This file is always injected as a high-priority system context regardless of token pressure. Your team shares it through the repository, so every engineer gets consistent Copilot behaviour from day one.
The common patterns that turn vague suggestions into Production code
After context, prompt quality is your biggest lever. Good prompts eliminate back-and-forth iterations. Bad prompts produce code you spend more time fixing than writing from scratch.
Follow the 3S principle: Specific, Simple, Short. A prompt like “fix the bug” gives Copilot nothing to work with. A prompt like “/explain why does this SQLAlchemy query return duplicate rows when joining orders and items? Then /fix update the join condition to use a distinct subquery” tells it exactly what to investigate and what to change.
The comment-as-specification pattern is the most reliable technique for inline completions. Write a comment that defines the function’s purpose, inputs, outputs, constraints, and edge cases. Then start the function signature. Copilot reads the comment as a spec and generates code that satisfies it.
def update_task(task_id: int, payload: TaskUpdateSchema) -> TaskResponse:
"""
Validate and update a task record in the database.
Args:
task_id: The ID of the task to update. Must exist.
payload: Partial task data. Only provided fields are updated.
Returns:
The updated TaskResponse with all fields populated.
Raises:
HTTPException: 404 if the task does not exist.
ValueError: If required fields are empty after merging.
"""
Use inline examples to establish patterns. Write the first instance of a repetitive pattern yourself, then let Copilot continue:
# status filter
if filters.status:
query = query.filter(Task.status == filters.status)
# Now generate similar filters for priority, category_id
Copilot will follow the exact structure: the same conditional shape, the same attribute access pattern, the same variable naming.
Test-Driven Development works remarkably well with Copilot. When you write a failing test first, the test acts as both a formal specification and an automatic validator. Write a pytest test like this:
def test_get_task_statistics_returns_correct_counts(db_session):
# Arrange: seed 2 pending, 1 in_progress, 1 done tasks for user 42
# Act: call get_task_statistics(user_id=42, db=db_session)
# Assert: result.pending == 2, result.in_progress == 1, result.done == 1
Then open the implementation file and start typing the function signature. Copilot sees the test in a neighbouring tab, reads the Arrange/Act/Assert structure, understands the return shape, and generates an implementation that satisfies the assertions. Running pytest confirms it. This workflow produces code that is verifiable and self-documenting by design.
Python-Specific Techniques that dramatically improve suggestion quality
There is one Python-specific habit that separates consistent Copilot output from generic boilerplate.
Type hints are not just documentation in Python. They are Copilot’s primary signal for understanding the shape of your data. A function with no annotations gets generic suggestions. Add Pydantic models as input and output types, and Copilot generates code that handles validation, serialization, and error handling correctly.
Without annotations:
def create_order(data):
# Copilot generates: order = Order(**data) with no validation
With annotations:
def create_order(payload: OrderCreateSchema) -> OrderResponse:
# Copilot generates validation against the Pydantic schema,
# calls .model_dump() correctly, and returns the right response type
The difference in output quality is significant. Annotate every function signature before you start the body.
Google-style docstrings are another high-value context signal. Write the docstring before the function body. Include Args, Returns, and Raises sections. Copilot reads the Raises section and generates the exact exception handling logic you described.
def get_order(order_id: int, user_id: int) -> OrderResponse:
"""
Retrieve a single order by ID, scoped to the authenticated user.
Args:
order_id: The ID of the order to retrieve.
user_id: The ID of the authenticated user. Orders belonging
to other users return 404, not 403.
Returns:
An OrderResponse with all fields populated.
Raises:
HTTPException: 404 if the order does not exist or belongs
to a different user.
"""
This docstring tells Copilot to generate a query that filters by both order_id and user_id, and to raise a 404 instead of a 403 when the record belongs to another user. Without the docstring, Copilot generates a query that filters only by order_id and exposes other users’ data.
pytest fixtures are the Python equivalent of the test-first pattern. Write the fixture and the test body before writing any implementation. Copilot sees the fixture in conftest.py, understands the data shape, and generates an implementation that satisfies the test assertions.
# conftest.py
@pytest.fixture
def sample_task(db_session):
return Task(id=1, user_id=42, status="pending", title="Write tests")
# test_task_service.py
def test_get_task_returns_task_for_owner(sample_task, db_session):
result = get_task(task_id=1, user_id=42, db=db_session)
assert result.id == 1
assert result.status == "pending"
def test_get_task_raises_404_for_wrong_user(sample_task, db_session):
with pytest.raises(HTTPException) as exc:
get_task(task_id=1, user_id=99, db=db_session)
assert exc.value.status_code == 404
Open conftest.py and test_task_service.py in tabs, then start typing the get_task function signature in task_service.py. Copilot reads both the fixture and the test assertions, understands the user-scoping requirement, and generates an implementation that raises 404 for a mismatched user_id. The test is the spec.
Inline Completions vs Chat: Knowing which mode to use saves 30 minutes a day
Copilot offers two interaction modes, and knowing when to use each eliminates friction.
Use inline completions when you are writing straightforward code with clear patterns: CRUD operations, boilerplate, extending an existing code pattern, or completing a function where the signature makes intent obvious. Inline completions are fast and require no context switching. The first suggestion is not always the best. Accept partial suggestions with Ctrl+Right Arrow when the first 70–80% of a suggestion is correct, and the rest needs a small edit.
Use Copilot Chat when you need to understand unfamiliar code, debug errors, refactor complex logic across multiple files, ship new features or explore multiple implementation approaches. The Chat panel supports multi-turn conversations (ask | plan | agent), which is critical for complex tasks.
Key slash commands to build into your muscle memory:
- /explain: Get a detailed breakdown of selected code. Use this before /fix when debugging. Understanding the root cause leads to better fixes and prevents accumulating patches you do not understand.
- /fix: Debug and fix errors. Most effective after /explain establishes context about the root cause.
- /tests: Generate comprehensive test cases for a function or class.
- /doc: Create JSDoc or docstring documentation for selected code.
- /new: Scaffold a new file from a description. Describe what you need “a FastAPI router for user authentication with JWT and refresh tokens” and Copilot generates the file with the correct structure and imports scoped to your project context.
- /newNotebook: Generate a Jupyter notebook from a prompt. Copilot writes the imports, dataset loading, and exploratory scaffolding so you start from a working skeleton instead of a blank cell.
- /setupTests: Configure a testing framework from scratch. Copilot detects your language, installs the appropriate packages, and creates the initial configuration files. Run this once on a fresh repository before writing the first test.
- @workspace searches your entire codebase to answer a question. Ask it “how do we handle authentication in this codebase?” or “where is the JWT token validation logic?” and it finds the relevant files without manual searching. This saves hours when onboarding onto a new project.
- #file references a specific file: “Update #task_service.py to add pagination support.”
- #codebase tells Copilot to search automatically across relevant files. @workspace is underused by most engineers, and it is one of the most valuable features in the entire toolset.
The Three-Sentence Prompt That Writes a Full FastAPI Endpoint
Agent mode transforms Copilot from a reactive autocomplete tool into an autonomous coding agent. When you switch to Agent mode and send a prompt, Copilot enters a reasoning loop: it receives the task, generates an internal plan, selects tools (read_file, search_workspace, run_terminal, edit_file, get_errors), executes each step, monitors output for errors, self-corrects when something fails, and continues until the task is complete.
Agent mode is the right choice for scaffolding new features, refactoring across files, setting up test infrastructure, migrating testing frameworks, and automating multi-step tasks. For a concrete workflow: describe a new FastAPI endpoint with JWT middleware and Copilot writes the router, the service layer, the Pydantic schemas, the pytest fixtures, and registers the router in main.py, without you touching a single file manually. It reads linter errors and failed tests after each step, fixes them, reruns pytest, and iterates until all checks pass.
The prompt that triggers this is two or three specific sentences, not a paragraph:
Add a POST /tasks endpoint to the tasks router. Protect it with JWT authentication
using the get_current_user dependency already defined in auth.py. The handler should
accept a TaskCreateSchema body, call create_task in task_service.py, and return
TaskResponse. Write pytest tests in tests/test_tasks.py using the db_session and
authenticated_client fixtures from conftest.py.
Copilot reads the existing `auth.py`, `task_service.py`, and `conftest.py`, follows their patterns exactly, then writes each file, runs pytest, and fixes any failures before it stops.
Beyond local agent mode, the GitHub Coding Agent lets you assign any issue directly to Copilot from the GitHub UI: it spins up an isolated environment, writes the code, runs your CI, and opens a draft PR while you work on something else. Multiple sessions run in parallel and appear in the Agents view so you can track all of them from one place, including from GitHub Mobile.
The GitHub Copilot CLI extends this with `/delegate`. After building a plan in a terminal CLI session outlining steps, scoping files, sketching the approach type `/delegate` and the entire conversation plus any local context is pushed to the GitHub Coding Agent in the cloud. A new branch and draft PR appear immediately. You close the terminal and keep working locally on something else. The PR logs stream in real time. When the agent finishes, you get mentioned in the PR and it is ready for review. The practical value: any long-running refactor or upgrade task you have mapped out locally can be handed off in one command instead of blocking your terminal for an hour.
A second agent prompt pattern worth adding to your workflow handles cross-file refactoring:
Migrate all permission checks in the tasks and orders routers to use the
require_role("member") dependency defined in auth/dependencies.py. Remove any
inline permission logic that duplicates this pattern. Run pytest after each
file changes and fix any failures before moving to the next file.
Agent mode handles this without manual coordination: it reads the `require_role` pattern once, applies it across every router file, and self-corrects on test failures before moving on.
MCP Servers:
How to Give Copilot Access to Your Database, Browser, and GitHub?
Model Context Protocol (MCP) is an open standard backed by Anthropic, Microsoft, Google, Amazon, and OpenAI. It provides a unified interface for AI models to use external tools. Think of it as a standardized connector: you set up a Postgres MCP server once, and any MCP-compatible client (Copilot, Claude Code, Cursor) queries your database through the same interface.
MCP servers give Copilot real-time access to databases, APIs, file systems, and services that live outside your codebase.
The servers you should configure:
- GitHub MCP Server: The most essential server for any GitHub user. Over 100 tools for listing issues, creating branches, managing pull requests, searching repositories and code, and assigning issues to the Coding Agent, all from Copilot Chat.
- Filesystem MCP Server: Provides granular, permission-controlled file system access. The agent reads, writes, searches, and manages directories with explicit permission scoping. Essential for refactoring workflows that touch code outside the current workspace.
- Playwright MCP Server: Browser automation for end-to-end testing and UI validation. Copilot navigates pages, clicks buttons, fills forms, takes screenshots, and verifies visual changes. The Coding Agent has Playwright installed by default and uses it to verify UI changes before marking a task complete.
- Database MCP Servers: Postgres, Supabase, Prisma, SQLite, MongoDB. Query schemas and data with natural language. Ask “which users signed up last week” and get SQL results. Start with read-only access for production databases.
- Sequential Thinking MCP Server: Externalizes the AI’s reasoning as explicit steps and branches instead of opaque single-step answers. Improves quality for architecture migrations, complex debugging, and large refactors.
- Context7 MCP Server: Pulls up-to-date library documentation directly into Copilot’s context. When you need current API references that post-date the model’s training data, Context7 retrieves them in real time.
- Memory MCP Server: Knowledge graph-based persistent memory across sessions. Stores structured understanding of people, codebases, and project context. For long-running projects where session context loss is a friction point.
Configure MCP servers in your .vscode/mcp.json file:
{
"servers": {
"github":
{
"type": "http",
"url": "https://api.githubcopilot.com/mcp/"
},
"context7":
{
"type": "stdio",
"command": "npx",
"args": ["@upstash/context7-mcp@1.0.31"],
"env":
{
"CONTEXT7_API_KEY": "${input:CONTEXT7_API_KEY}"
}
},
"postgres":
{
"command": "npx",
"args": ["-y", "@modelcontextprotocol/server-postgres",
"postgresql://user:password@localhost:5432/mydb"]
},
"playwright":
{
"command": "npx",
"args": ["-y", "@playwright/mcp@latest", " - browser", "chrome"]
}
}
}
Never hardcode secrets in mcp.json. Use the inputs array for credentials. Start with read-only MCP servers for production databases. Add a rule to your copilot-instructions.md: “When querying the database, use the read-only MCP tool instead of direct SQL.”
Python-based MCP servers use uvx or python -m instead of npx. For example, the markitdown server runs as uvx markitdown-mcp rather than an npx command. The command and args fields in mcp.json work the same way regardless of runtime, so the configuration structure is identical.
The VS Code Setup That Makes AI-Generated Code Production-Ready
Copilot generates code. These extensions ensure that code is correct, clean, and production-ready.
- Error Lens surfaces compiler errors, lint warnings, and type errors inline. It flags issues in AI-generated code instantly, without hovering. When an error appears, you trigger /fix immediately. This is the single most valuable extension to pair with Copilot.
- GitLens shows inline blame annotations, commit history, and authorship on every line. Essential for understanding the context of AI-generated code in team projects and for reviewing Copilot Agent’s commits before merging.
- Pylance provides fast type checking, IntelliSense, and import resolution for Python. It surfaces type errors in Copilot-generated code before you run anything — the faster you catch a type mismatch, the cheaper it is to fix.
- Ruff replaces flake8, isort, and black in a single fast tool. It lints and formats on save, so Copilot’s output is automatically corrected to your style rules without a second step. Set it as your default Python formatter in settings.json with `”[python]”: { “editor.defaultFormatter”: “charliermarsh.ruff” }`.
- mypy runs static type analysis on every save. When Copilot generates code that passes the wrong type to a function, mypy catches it inline. Pair it with `strict = true` in your pyproject.toml to get maximum signal on generated code.
- REST Client lets you test APIs directly in VS Code using .http files. Ask Copilot to generate request files for your endpoints and test them without leaving the editor.
- TODO Tree scans your codebase for TODO and FIXME comments. Copilot picks up these comments as context, so you walk through TODOs one at a time and let Copilot resolve each one.
- Code Spell Checker catches spelling mistakes in Copilot-generated comments and variable names before they make it to production.
For VS Code settings, configure these in your settings.json:
- Enable Copilot per language: turn it off for plain text and YAML where suggestions are rarely useful. Turn it on for your primary languages by setting github.copilot.enable with per-language keys.
- Enable inline suggestions: set editor.inlineSuggest.enabled to true for ghost text completions.
- Enable Next Edit Suggestions: set github.copilot.nextEditSuggestions.enabled to true. NES predicts where your next edit needs to happen after you make a change and navigates you there. Press Tab to jump to the suggested edit and Tab again to accept it.
- Enable instruction files: set github.copilot.chat.codeGeneration.useInstructionFiles to true so Copilot reads your .github/copilot-instructions.md automatically.
Here is the complete settings.json block for all four settings at once:
{
"github.copilot.enable": {
"python": true,
"javascript": true,
"yaml": false,
"plaintext": false
},
"editor.inlineSuggest.enabled": true,
"github.copilot.nextEditSuggestions.enabled": true,
"github.copilot.chat.codeGeneration.useInstructionFiles": true
}
Use VS Code Profiles to create different extension sets for different projects. A “FastAPI Profile” with Pylance, Ruff, REST Client, and Error Lens versus a “Data Science Profile” with Pylance, Jupyter, and Black keeps resource usage low and extensions relevant to the current task.
The Configuration File That Gives Every Engineer on Your Team Better Suggestions
The .github/copilot-instructions.md file is the most underused configuration in Copilot. It is read automatically with every request as high-priority system context. Engineers who set it up once get consistently better suggestions for the lifetime of the project.
What to include:
- your tech stack (languages, frameworks, database, testing libraries)
- coding patterns (functional vs OOP, async style, layering rules)
- naming conventions (variables, functions, files, classes)
- error handling approach (error types, HTTP status code conventions)
- testing style (framework, mocking strategy, test naming conventions)
- security rules (environment variables, input validation requirements)
- documentation style (JSDoc format, inline comment preferences).
For an existing project: switch Copilot to Agent mode and type exactly this prompt:
“Scan this repository and generate a .github/copilot-instructions.md file based on the tech stack, coding patterns, naming conventions, and testing setup you observe.”
Copilot reads your imports, your existing service structure, your test fixtures, and your error handling style, then writes a working file. Review and refine it manually. This bootstraps the file in under two minutes.
Beyond instructions, prompt files (.github/prompts/) store reusable AI prompts as markdown files. A code-review.prompt.md file defines your review checklist. A security-audit.prompt.md file specifies what to check for. Team members invoke these with slash commands in chat. Prompt files support front matter where you specify the mode (ask, agent) and even the model to use, which means a memory-related prompt automatically switches to a cheaper model instead of burning premium requests.
Here is what a `.github/prompts/security-audit.prompt.md` file looks like in practice:
Review the selected code for security vulnerabilities. Check for:
- Hardcoded secrets or API keys (flag any string that looks like a token, password, or key)
- SQL injection: raw string concatenation in queries instead of parameterized inputs
- Missing input validation on user-supplied data before it reaches business logic
- Missing authorization: verify the current user owns the resource before returning it
- Unhandled exceptions that could expose stack traces or internal error details
- HTTP 200 responses returned on error paths
For each issue, show the vulnerable line and the corrected version side by side.
Prompt files can also encode multi-stage interactive workflows. Here is a `.github/prompts/plan-review.prompt.md` that runs a structured architecture and code quality review, pausing for your input at each stage before continuing:
Before starting, ask the user which review depth they want:
**1/ BIG CHANGE** - Work through this interactively, one section at a time
(Architecture → Code Quality → Tests → Performance) with at most 4 top issues per section.
**2/ SMALL CHANGE** - Work through interactively, ONE question per review section.
Wait for the user to choose before proceeding.
For each section below, output your findings THEN pause and ask for feedback before
moving to the next section. NUMBER every issue. Give LETTERS to options (A, B, C).
Always put your recommended option first, labelled clearly. Format each issue as:
**Issue N - [title]**
*Problem:* Concrete description with file/line reference.
*Option A (Recommended):* [approach] - effort / risk / maintenance impact
*Option B:* [approach] - effort / risk / maintenance impact
*Option C - Do nothing:* [rationale and risk of inaction]
*My recommendation:* Option A because [reason tied to user preferences].
Then ask: "Do you agree with Option A, or would you prefer B or C for Issue N?"
## Section 1 - Architecture Review
Evaluate: overall system design and component boundaries, dependency graph and
coupling concerns, data flow patterns and bottlenecks, scaling characteristics
and single points of failure, security architecture (auth, data access, API
boundaries). Surface at most 4 issues. After presenting them, ask: "Ready to
move to Code Quality, or do you want to discuss any of these issues first?"
## Section 2 - Code Quality Review
Evaluate: code organisation and module structure, DRY violations (flag
repetition aggressively), error handling patterns and missing edge cases (call
these out explicitly), technical debt hotspots, areas that are over-engineered
or under-engineered. Surface at most 4 issues. Pause for feedback before
continuing.
## Section 3 - Test Review
Evaluate: coverage gaps (unit, integration, e2e), assertion strength, missing
edge case coverage, untested failure modes and error paths. Bias toward
identifying too few tests rather than too many. Surface at most 4 issues.
Pause for feedback before continuing.
## Section 4 - Performance Review
Evaluate: N+1 queries and database access patterns, memory-usage concerns,
caching opportunities, slow or high-complexity code paths. Surface at most 4
issues. After all sections are complete, summarise the total issue list with
their chosen resolutions.
Guiding principles for all recommendations:
- DRY is important - flag repetition aggressively.
- Well-tested code is non-negotiable; err toward more tests.
- Aim for "engineered enough" - not fragile/hacky and not prematurely abstract.
- Handle more edge cases, not fewer. Thoughtfulness over speed.
- Prefer explicit over clever.
- Do not assume priorities on timeline or scale. Ask if relevant.
You can also define custom agents (formerly custom modes) using markdown files in .github/agents/. A custom agent overrides the default Copilot behavior with a specific identity and workflow. For example, a “plan” agent that first researches the codebase, then presents a concise plan, handles feedback, and offers to implement, or a “security reviewer” agent that only looks for vulnerabilities and hardcoded secrets.
The Six-Point Security Checklist for Every Block of AI-Generated Code
Every line of AI-generated code becomes your responsibility once merged. These six checks should be non-negotiable:
- Parameterized queries. Copilot sometimes generates raw SQL with string concatenation or template literals containing user input. Always verify that database queries use parameterized inputs or ORM methods.
- No hardcoded secrets. Copilot frequently suggests hardcoded API keys, passwords, or tokens for convenience. Replace every one with an environment variable reference and validate that the variable exists at startup.
- Input validation. User input gets validated before it touches business logic or database operations. Copilot generates the happy path well but often omits validation for edge cases, malformed data, or missing required fields.
- Correct HTTP status codes. Verify that error responses return appropriate status codes (400 for bad input, 401 for unauthenticated, 403 for unauthorized, 404 for not found, 500 for server errors), not a blanket 200 or 500 for everything.
- Try-catch coverage. HTTP endpoints need try-catch blocks or centralized error handlers. Copilot sometimes generates only the success path. Unhandled exceptions crash your server or leak sensitive stack traces.
- No over-permissive access patterns. Check that middleware, role checks, and authorization guards are present and correct. Copilot does not understand your authorization model unless you specify it.
Code types to always write or closely supervise yourself: authentication logic, authorization checks, cryptographic implementations, payment processing, anything handling PII, and performance-sensitive algorithms on hot code paths.
The Eight Mistakes That Kill Copilot Effectiveness, Ranked by How Often Engineers Make Them
- Accepting the first suggestion without cycling alternatives. This is the most common mistake. Use Option+] (Mac) or Alt+] (Windows/Linux) to cycle through 2–3 alternatives. The second or third option is often better than the first, especially for logic-heavy functions. Three hours of debugging will trace back to this.
- Never setting up copilot-instructions.md. This is permanent, zero-effort context improvement that works on every single request for the lifetime of the project. It takes fifteen minutes to set up and pays dividends daily. Engineers who skip it are choosing to get worse suggestions indefinitely.
- Using one-word or one-line prompts. “Create a service” produces a generic function with no type annotations, no error handling, and no alignment to your data layer. “FastAPI service function to fetch paginated tasks for a user, using SQLAlchemy async session, returning a list of TaskResponse Pydantic models, raising HTTPException 404 if user not found” produces code that fits your architecture.
- Having too many unrelated files open. Close everything from last session before starting a new task. Copilot’s context window is limited and every irrelevant file dilutes it.
- Using inline completions for complex multi-file logic. Switch to Chat with @workspace or Agent mode for anything that spans multiple files or requires reasoning about the broader codebase.
- Not verifying generated error handling. Copilot generates the happy path well. It regularly omits try-catch blocks, input validation, and edge case handling.
- Copy-pasting Copilot output into production without understanding it. If you cannot explain what a block of code does and why it is correct, do not merge it. Every accepted suggestion should pass the test: “Would I have written this myself given enough time?”
- Not using @workspace for codebase questions. Engineers manually search files for patterns when @workspace finds them in seconds.
The engineers who extract the most value from Copilot share one trait: they treat it as a collaborator that responds to specificity. Vague instructions produce vague code. Precise context, structured comments, a well-configured environment, and intentional prompt patterns produce code that matches your architecture, follows your conventions, and handles your edge cases. Open your repository and add a .github/copilot-instructions.md file today. Everything else follows from that.
